articles.title: Google Declaring War on the Web
articles.summary: Google's declaration of war on the web, announced in an article published by tante in English, is a significant development that threatens the foundational structure of the internet. The company is pushing search algorithms to focus more on providing answers directly to users rather than linking them back to sources. This shift away from traditional human-computer interaction has led Google to establish a new abstraction layer on top of their existing search engine, creating a web environment where information is processed and generated by large language models (LLMs). The core issue with this approach is that it erases the distinction between content creators and consumers, making it difficult for users to distinguish between legitimate sources and those that are not. This has led to a new paradigm of "here's your processed answer" versus "AI Overviews," where search results are generated by LLMs rather than human authors. The article suggests that this approach is aimed at establishing a new abstraction layer on the web, one that Google controls and moderates.

articles.title: PopuLoRA: Co-Evolving LLM Populations for Reasoning Self- Play
articles.summary:
The PopuLoRA project aims to co-evolve large language models (LLMs) by allowing them to learn from each other. By sharing their knowledge and experiences, the PopuLoRA team hopes to improve LLMs' ability to reason self-play, a key challenge in natural language processing. This co-evolutionary approach is crucial for developers as it enables them to leverage the strengths of multiple models and adapt to new contexts more effectively.
The PopuLoRA project uses a novel method called "coevolutionary learning" to achieve this goal. The team combines LLMs' knowledge with their own experiences, allowing them to learn from each other's strengths and weaknesses. This approach is particularly effective for reasoning self-play, as it enables the models to adapt to new contexts by incorporating information from both sides.
The PopuLoRA project has already made some progress in this area, but there are still challenges to overcome.

articles.title: Not alive, but not dead: disembodied human brains used for drug testing
articles.summary: Not alive, but not dead: disembodied human brains used for drug testing. Developers and researchers are increasingly turning to artificial intelligence (AI) technologies like deep learning, natural language processing, and computer vision to analyze large datasets of medical records and patient data. One such AI technology is the use of disembodied human brains in drug testing. By using these brain scans, researchers can quickly identify potential drug candidates that may be effective for certain diseases or conditions. This approach allows for faster and more efficient drug discovery processes compared to traditional methods that require extensive laboratory experiments. However, it also raises concerns about the ethical implications of using AI technologies to analyze human data, such as privacy and bias issues. Developers are increasingly exploring ways to ensure that these technologies are used responsibly and ethically.

articles.summary: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is a common security vulnerability that can be exploited to steal user credentials. While passkeys are an effective way to store and verify authentication tokens, they also expose users to the risk of XSS attacks. The article explains how attackers can use JavaScript to register a passkey without the website's control, leading to a persistent account takeover backdoor. It highlights the importance of implementing strong attestation mechanisms to protect against such vulnerabilities and emphasizes that while passkeys bring benefits, there is a gap in the threat model that needs to be addressed by developers.

articles.title: Logic bug in the Linux kernel's __ptrace_may_access() function (CVE-2026-46333)
articles.summary: The Linux kernel's __ptrace_may_access() function is vulnerable to a logic bug that allows an attacker to access arbitrary system files and execute commands as root. This vulnerability is locally exploitable for information disclosure and arbitrary command execution as root, making it a critical security issue for developers working with the Linux operating system. The article provides four different exploits for this vulnerability: chage (a set-uid-root or set-gid-shadow binary), ssh-keysign (a set-uid-root binary), pkexec (a set-uid-root binary), and accounts-daemon (a root daemon).

articles.title: Qian Xuesen: The missile genius America lost and China gained (2025)
articles.summary:
Qian Xuesen, a Chinese-American physicist and mathematician, is known for his contributions to nuclear physics. He was an early pioneer in the field of high-energy physics and played a significant role in the development of the H-bomb. Qian Xuesen's work laid the foundation for the successful development of China's first atomic bomb in 1964.

The article highlights that while Qian Xuesen is remembered as a brilliant scientist, he was also a key figure in the Cold War and played a crucial role in the United States' nuclear arsenal. His contributions to high-energy physics were instrumental in developing the H-bomb, which led to China's acquisition of its first atomic bomb. This article emphasizes that Qian Xuesen's legacy is not just about his scientific achievements but also about his role in shaping the course of history and ensuring the security of his country.

articles.title: SBCL: the ultimate assembly code breadboard (2014)
articles.summary:
SBCL (System Lisp) introduces a new feature called NEXT, which is used to encode an effective address with an index register but no base. The mistake doesn't affect the meaning of the instruction, but forces a wasteful encoding. The key technology here is the use of modular counters and stack-based VMs that restrict their stack size to 8 slots.
The article highlights the importance of SBCL for developers who need to work with assembly code and machine code interchangeably. It shows how the NEXT sequence can be used to encode addresses without affecting the meaning, but it forces a wasteful encoding by requiring a base register. This is important because it affects how code is executed in different programming environments.
The article also touches on the limitations of x87 registers and the potential for stack-based VMs that restrict their stack size to 8 slots. It suggests that developers should consider using specialized primitives for all 8 values the stack counter can take, rather than hardcoding addresses in bytecode.

articles.title: the may 2026 fedi software vulnerability
articles.summary: The May 2026 Fedi software vulnerability involves a potential security issue where ld-signatures are not properly validated and expanded to their full capacity. This can lead to a flaw in how Linked Data Signatures (LD-Signatures) work, allowing attackers to manipulate data without proper authorization. The key finding is that ld-signatures rely on RDF triples and graphs, while activitystreams use plain JSON with some quirks. This mismatch makes ld-signatures vulnerable to attacks like the one described, where an attacker can re-order properties in a malicious object signed by a victim's signature. Developers should consider applying security updates promptly to mitigate this vulnerability.

articles.title: Erasing Existentials
articles.summary:
Erasing Existentials is a programming language feature that allows developers to use existential quantifiers in their code. The key technology here is the concept of "dyn Trait", which represents an existential type with a specific trait. This means that while Rust's type system guarantees that any value of type dyn Trait conforms to its underlying type, it does not provide information about what values of type dyn Trait might be. To address this, we can use the "for-exists conversion" technique, which allows us to express existential types in a way that is more explicit and easier to reason about.

The article explains how to use the for-exists conversion to write an existential type S: Trait<B> where B is another existentially quantified type variable. It also discusses the benefits of using these techniques in Rust's type system, such as making it easier to reason about code and improving performance by avoiding unnecessary type conversions.

articles.title: Saying Goodbye to Asm.js
articles.summary: Axe-time, sword-time, shields are sundered, Wind-time, wolf-time, ere the world falls. – Völuspá , Poetic Edda As of Firefox 148, SpiderMonkey's asm.js optimizations are disabled by default and we plan to remove the code entirely in a future release. If you maintain a site that uses asm.js, nothing will break. asm.js is just a subset of plain JavaScript, so the code keeps running through our regular JIT just like any other script. That said, recompiling to WebAssembly will get you faster execution and smaller binaries. History: asm.js was Mozilla’s response to the question posed by NaCl and PNaCl : how can the web run code at native speeds? The idea was clever: pick a strict, statically-typed subset of JavaScript that an engine could recognize on the fly and compile down to native code.
